owner_choice_model.purpose: Route each durable concern to one primary owner surface before adding or trusting another copy.
review_questions: Is this a stable repo-wide policy, a behavior contract, current execution state, durable understanding, review evidence, generated mirror, package payload, or runtime primitive?
Which surface is allowed to be edited directly, and which surfaces must be regenerated, archived, or treated as derived?
Would putting this fact in a second visible place create duplicate authority instead of a useful adapter or proof?

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is a stable machine-readable repo default, posture, or workflow obligation.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern is only active-slice state, historical evidence, or implementation behavior.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern defines a reusable payload, schema, command contract, proof route, or generation source.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern needs live filesystem inspection or imperative runtime behavior.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is current continuation, active work, roadmap ordering, or slice-local execution state.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern should remain true after the active lane closes without promotion.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is reusable repo understanding or learned context that informs future interpretation.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern is a binding policy, active task, or validation rule.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is bounded review output, finding evidence, or issue-intake history.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern has been accepted as current plan, durable memory, or enforceable contract.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is a generated mirror or runnable adapter derived from contracts.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern is the source contract or hand-written runtime primitive.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern is installed product-managed payload, managed fence content, or upgrade-replaceable module state.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern belongs to repo-owned startup instructions or local runtime implementation.

concern_classes.route_when: The concern needs imperative behavior, live repo inspection, subprocess execution, or mutation sequencing.
concern_classes.avoid_when: The concern can be expressed as a declarative contract, schema, or package payload.

areas.notes: Stable compact answer envelope shape and selector metadata are manifest-backed.
Stable proof route ids and commands are manifest-backed.
Changed-path proof classification rules and supplemental proof lanes are manifest-backed so bounded implementer proof selection does not depend on inline Python path-policy tables.

Authority marker labels, canonical source policy, safe-to-edit defaults, refresh commands, and representative parity paths are manifest-backed while runtime path normalization and matching stay Python-owned.
Stable report schema metadata is manifest-backed.
Repo config shape is now documented as a formal schema in addition to parser enforcement.
Local mixed-agent override shape is now documented as a formal schema in addition to parser enforcement.
The optional setup/jumpstart findings artifact shape is formalized separately so the workspace can standardize promotion without owning the analysis method.
Local delegation outcome evidence shape is formalized separately from the editable local override so accumulated tuning evidence stays quiet and machine-readable.
The bounded startup context payload emitted by the CLI is schema-backed so adapter consumers can validate the shape without treating cli.py as the sole contract.
The bounded implementer context payload emitted by the CLI is schema-backed so changed-path handoffs and proof requirements can be validated by non-Python adapters.
Stable startup and implementer context text fragments are contract-backed so cli.py remains the renderer instead of the wording authority.
Combined workspace report payload is derived from manifests, config, and live repo state.
Defaults still assemble declarative and procedural contract answers into one emitted payload.
Lifecycle execution stays Python-owned in the first tranche.
Reconciliation and drift decisions still depend on live repo state and remain procedural.
The internal module capability contract is now formalized as a schema to reduce prose dependency.

The module registry declares root workspace components plus module-owned MCP-style resources, tools, prompts, schemas, roots, safety metadata, and future adapter boundaries without adding an MCP runtime dependency.
Stable workspace-owned path tables and setup artifact locations are manifest-backed instead of duplicated in cli.py.
Setup findings promotion thresholds and accepted class policy are manifest-backed instead of duplicated in cli.py.
Workflow artifact adapter profiles now come from checked-in contract JSON instead of inline Python tables.
Workflow component structure, composition, attachment, and migration posture are schema-backed so workflow docs can stay adapters over a machine-usable format.
Improvement latitude mode policy text and evidence metadata are manifest-backed instead of inline Python tables.
Optimization bias mode policy text and surface-boundary metadata are manifest-backed instead of inline Python tables.
Repo-friction policy payloads and boundary tests are manifest-backed instead of inline Python tables.
High-risk command gating, preflight token format, token freshness defaults, and strict-gate messages are manifest-backed instead of inline Python constants.
Draft operation contracts describe behavior, effects, guards, proof expectations, and primitive sequencing without replacing current runtime logic yet.

consumers: future Python and TypeScript target executors
areas.notes: The portable IR layer is limited to target-executor primitives over rooted paths, package payloads, JSON/TOML/Markdown, records, schemas, templates, and emitted output. Package-semantic primitives remain explicit runtime capabilities until they are decomposed behind this smaller substrate.
Black-box conformance contracts validate alternate or generated implementations against operation behavior without trusting their implementation path.

Generator-facing adapter bindings join command shape, operation contracts, primitive refs, schemas, projection hints, and conformance refs while leaving runtime primitive behavior implementation-owned.

consumers: future Python and TypeScript package generators
areas.notes: Implementation-independent package view joins generated command adapters into target package definitions so Python and TypeScript outputs can derive from the same contract without reading CLI implementation code.
The remaining Python-owned extractable content is classified by future owner, authority status, readiness, and follow-on slice before more extraction work proceeds.
The Python contract-consumption boundary, validated-loader route, and schema-derived model policy are explicit contract data rather than implicit helper behavior.
Remaining Python-owned behavior is classified as procedural-owned, live-inspection-owned, operation-contract-covered, or deferred-extractable so cli.py is no longer the only boundary map.
Module metadata, command args, and result-contract fields are manifest-backed while lifecycle handlers and detectors remain procedural.
Command names, parser help text, and command-local options are manifest-backed instead of duplicated in argparse construction.
Shared parser option groups are manifest-backed instead of duplicated in helper functions.
SkillSpec records define behavior, CLI affordances, interpreted output fields, owned surfaces, proportionality, fallback, proof, and closeout obligations without starting generated target work.
